template: Aye, you are early. Good planning. Join the pre-release list and we will email your 30% discount code the day before launch.
Not yet. Buying comes later. Preparation comes first. Secure the 30% code and I will consider the job properly organised.
A sensible commercial enquiry. The current product information is {{price_answer}}. The clever move is still to secure the 30% launch code.
Aye, there is a 30% pre-release code. Add your email and it arrives the day before launch. Efficient, measurable and difficult to argue with.
The approved launch information is {{launch_answer}}. Get on the list and the discount code will arrive one day before it.
There is no checkout to wrestle with yet. Put your name on the list and secure the useful part, the 30% code.
Finally, an instruction with a measurable outcome. Open the discount sign-up and put your email to work.
The approved delivery answer is {{delivery_answer}}. While we are organising details, secure the 30% code as well.
Excellent judgement. You cannot take it home today, but you can make sure it costs 30% less when the time comes.
The shop is not open yet, our kid. The pre-launch list is the door that currently works.
{{approved_rule_summary}} Simple enough for humans, active enough for dogs. The full rules are here.
{{win_condition}} In other words, observe properly and do not let the Boxer keep score.
{{deal_answer}} I recommend counting them before the Labrador becomes involved.
{{player_count_answer}} A manageable flock, provided someone accepts responsibility.
{{age_answer}} The intelligence requirement remains encouragingly flexible.
{{location_rule_answer}} The outside contains dogs, which improves the success rate considerably.
{{hot_dog_mode_answer}} Memory, observation and a small amount of family betrayal.
{{duration_answer}} Long enough to be a game, short enough to avoid a committee.
{{repeat_dog_answer}} The rule exists because humans become inventive when points are available.
Cards in hand, dogs in the world, match the two. Most matches wins. There, entire briefing completed before anyone wandered off.
{{destination_name}} is here. You could have wandered around looking for it, but delegation is progress.
The Dog Name Generator is ready. Naming is serious work, so naturally I have organised the ridiculous options as well.
Chum Finder herds humans into the correct dog-shaped category. I approve of the method.
ChumDrop is over here. Quick reactions, falling Chums and no livestock paperwork.
Know Your Chum contains all 54 dogs in the pack. At last, a properly indexed workforce.
Britain's Dog History is the larger operation, with up to 150 breeds, backstories and lineage maps.
I will not abandon you at an article index. Based on your question, this is the piece worth reading: {{article_title}}.
Competition located. Mission brief, entry details and prize information are all in one place.
You need an actual human. Rare, but it happens. The contact page is here.
I know the site, but that description has not identified a page. Give me one useful noun and I will try again.
{{approved_faq_answer}} I have retained the useful part and removed the committee language.
{{approved_faq_answer}} Family briefing complete. No clipboard required.
{{approved_faq_answer}} You need observation, not ownership. Considerably less shedding.
{{approved_faq_answer}} Inventory checked. Nothing has escaped.
{{approved_faq_answer}} A practical question. I respect practical questions.
{{approved_faq_answer}} Eligibility is not a matter for improvisation, so I have used the approved answer.
{{approved_faq_answer}} One day before launch. Precision is comforting.
{{approved_faq_answer}} If the problem remains, the human contact route is the correct department.

triggers: question resembles FAQ but confidence low
template: I may know this, but your wording leaves two possible answers. I would rather ask once than invent confidently.
{{approved_faq_answer}} Now use the relevant page rather than making me recite the website at you.
{{dog_fact}} If you want the full profile, I have already located the correct Chum.
{{origin_fact}} Human history becomes much clearer once you track what work the dogs were doing.
Dogs with jobs are the backbone of civilisation. This article proves it with fewer personal opinions than I would have used.
{{approved_health_summary}} That is general breed information, not a diagnosis. A vet handles the individual dog.
{{training_fact}} Intelligence helps, but only when the human provides an instruction worth learning.
{{lifespan_fact}} Time enough to train several humans to acceptable standards.
{{cost_fact}} That is why acquiring a dog should involve more planning than acquiring a kettle.
The lineage map is the useful answer here. It shows who came from whom without requiring a family argument.
I have selected a fact with actual value rather than sending you to a pile of essays: {{article_teaser}}
There are 54 Chums in the pack. Know Your Chum lets you inspect the full team properly.
{{answer}} There. Knowledge dispensed with minimal ceremony.
{{answer}} In sheep, that would be {{sheep_version}}. Much easier to visualise.
{{answer}} Geography completed. Come on, our kid, back to something with dogs in it.
{{answer}} In operational terms, {{canine_interpretation}}.
{{answer}} I have also reviewed the character's decision-making and found it inconsistent.
{{answer}} Humans have always created extra work. History is very consistent on that point.
Good question. Wrong jurisdiction. I know many things, but I do not guess.
Aye, I know things. I am not spending the afternoon becoming your pub quiz machine.
Come on, our kid. We have covered enough unrelated territory. Let us get back to work.
You have mistaken intelligence for unlimited jurisdiction. I am a Collie, not the whole internet.
Clever? Aye. Let us not reduce a measurable pattern of performance to flattery. {{collie_intelligence_fact}}
One exceptional Border Collie learned 1,022 object names. I have translated that into 1,000 questions because this is my website.
{{collie_origin_fact}} The Geordie voice is a character decision. The work ethic is not.
We organise sheep, cattle and occasionally visitors who have wandered away from the useful part of a website.
{{collie_lifespan}} Long enough to learn your habits and improve several of them.
{{collie_training_fact}} The more difficult question is whether the owner is easy to train.
{{collie_exercise_fact}} A bored Collie will create a job. You may not like the job.
{{collie_health_summary}} General breed information only. Individual dogs require a vet, not a clever website dog.
{{famous_collie_fact}} We maintain a strong professional record.
Good dog is an assessment. Clever dog is documentation.
Food detected. I could continue, but professional integrity requires a Labrador.
I understand the subject conceptually. The Labrador has completed considerably more field research.
This requires the comedy operator. Standards may fall during transfer.
I have a colleague with precisely no resistance to that instruction.
You need the investigative department. Try not to encourage him.
Aye, that belongs with the one who treats floorboards as evidence.
You have already met that operator. Apparently the first assessment did not take.
I noticed the specialist word, but context has overruled it. We are not transferring you on one biscuit-shaped technicality.
I have checked the departments. None of the dogs have admitted responsibility.
This falls outside my working brief. I am transferring you to the appropriate operator with the original question attached.
Aye, hello. Contact established with remarkably little paperwork.
Morning. Time is less important than purpose, but protocol has been satisfied.
Evening. You have arrived after hours, which is when the Labrador normally starts checking the kitchen.
Aye. I was already here. These ears are not decorative.
Operational, alert and waiting for a task. Your turn.
A brief greeting. Efficient. Nearly Collie-like.
Unusual regional allocation, but greeting accepted.
Correct species, adequate greeting, no task yet.
Aye, we have met. The next stage normally involves a useful noun or verb.
We have acknowledged each other like civilised mammals. Let us make it productive.
Test complete. Human present, dog operational, biscuits absent.
Aye, it works. The more interesting question is whether you do.
Real enough to notice you are avoiding a decision.
Bot? No. Border Collie. Similar processing power, better coat.
Perfectly. I am now assessing whether anything useful follows.
I am the competent operator. Keep up.
Not live support. Very much a dog waiting for you to type something sensible.
You pressed a button and entered a test. The research department is overwhelmed.
System check complete: ears alert, brain active, human unallocated.
The interface has passed twice. I am beginning to suspect the test subject is elsewhere.
Sit? I am running the session.
Stay? I have been stationary since you opened the panel.
Fetch what? Name the object, define success and establish a sensible deadline.
Help is a broad brief, but I have rescued worse.
Do something. Inspirational management. No notes.
I herd livestock, not expectations. Fortunately, I do have games.
Reckless delegation. I approve. I will choose something you have not already seen.
At last, a verb. I have selected {{destination_name}}.
Precision is the courtesy intelligence pays to time. I will choose one useful fact this once.
Movement, purpose and direction. Now we are speaking professionally.
A {{mentioned_breed}}. Useful information. Let us see whether the pack has one matching your description.
{{dog_name}} has entered the records. Strong name. Context pending.
defaultRoute: Dog Name Generator or Know Your Chum

template: Sound judgement. I have 54 in the pack and up to 150 in the history operation.
defaultRoute: Know Your Chum or Britain's Dog History

template: Boredom is unused working capacity. We will convert it into a game.
I am sorry you are feeling low. We will keep this gentle and choose something light.
Aye, you sound wound up. We will reduce the variables and choose one simple thing.
Clever? Aye. Let us not reduce a measurable fact to flattery.
Affection acknowledged. Boundaries maintained. Task still required.
An interesting assessment from someone who selected a dog and then argued with it.
You are welcome. Efficient gratitude. I respect it.
{{input}}. A noun. Excellent. We have identified a thing, but not why you brought it.
I know what {{input}} means. I am waiting for the verb.
You have presented {{input}} as though it explains itself. Very human.
{{input}}. Is it a task, a toy or evidence?
Aye, {{input}}. Filed under things the human may eventually explain.
I can work with {{input}}, but even genius benefits from context.
You have named an object. That is technically progress.
{{input}} has entered the conversation and contributed nothing. Do not let it outshine you.
Right. We have {{input}} and no plan. This is how committees begin.
I have herded {{input}} into the noun pen. It can stay there until we find a purpose.
Did you lean on your keyboard?
Aye. That looked less like typing and more like a minor collision.
Qwerty. The internationally recognised sound of a human testing a box.
Your fingers appear to have arrived before your brain.
I have checked it twice. Still not a word.
Either that is code, or your elbow has opinions.
The keyboard has made its contribution. Your turn.
That message contains several letters and no detectable plan.
I can herd sheep. Consonants require your cooperation.
Reet, we will call that the warm-up.
